title: Hindered transport of spherical particles in cylindrical pores: The role of structural heterogeneity in rejection-permeability trade-offs
authors: Debanik Bhattacharjee, Yaniv Edery, Guy Z. Ramon
abstract: Membrane separations rely on balancing rejection and permeability. Extensive work has clarified how pore structure and operating conditions control each quantity in idealized or weakly heterogeneous systems. However, it remains unclear how this trade-off emerges in strongly heterogeneous media, where coupled distributions of pore and particle sizes shape the local balance between advection and diffusion and generate substantial variability in performance among distribution realizations. Here we present a steric hindered-transport framework for spherical particles in cylindrical pores that explicitly resolves both single and coupled dual heterogeneity in size distributions. We show that the ensemble-averaged rejection increases with the particle-pore aspect ratio $λ$ and with the Péclet number $Pe$, while advection enhances steric exclusion by up to $\sim$20\% at intermediate $λ$. Dual heterogeneity broadens the distribution of effective $Pe$, increases the variability and incidence of anomalous rejection trends, while systematically shifting the rejection-permeability trade-off toward higher permeability at fixed rejection. These results suggest that controlled heterogeneity can serve as a design lever to expand the attainable operating space for simultaneous high selectivity and high throughput.
summary: The paper investigates how the combined randomness of particle and pore sizes influences membrane separation efficiency. It finds that 'dual heterogeneity' can actually enhance permeability without sacrificing rejection, providing a new strategy for designing high-performance filters.
main_problem: Understanding how the simultaneous variability of both particle and pore sizes (dual heterogeneity) affects the fundamental trade-off between rejection (selectivity) and permeability (throughput) in membrane separations.
main_result: Dual heterogeneity shifts the rejection-permeability trade-off toward higher permeability at a fixed rejection level, suggesting that controlled structural heterogeneity can be used as a design lever to improve membrane performance.
method: A steric hindered-transport framework using ensemble-averaged analysis of 10,000 independently sampled particle-pore pairs, where radii are modeled using normal distributions.
model_or_system: Transport of spherical particles through cylindrical pores, governed by the competition between convection (advection) and diffusion, modeled via a steric hindered-transport framework.
key_observables: Ensemble-averaged rejection (chi), permeability (P), particle-pore aspect ratio (lambda), and Péclet number (Pe).
important_parameters: Particle-pore aspect ratio (lambda), Péclet number (Pe), pore thickness (L), and pressure drop (delta p).
main_assumptions: Pores are cylindrical, particles are spherical, transport follows Hagen-Poiseuille flow, and particles follow centerline-only trajectories without electrostatic or chemical interactions.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the transport setup and sampling windows; Fig 2 shows rejection vs. aspect ratio for monodisperse cases; Fig 3-4 illustrate single-heterogeneity ensembles; Fig 5-6 show dual-heterogeneity construction and response; Fig 7 compares permeability ratios between single and dual heterogeneity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the rejection-permeability trade-off, establishes a steric hindered-transport model, compares monodisperse, single-heterogeneity, and dual-heterogeneity systems, and concludes with the design implications of structural heterogeneity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Schematic of single-pass transport of spherical particles through a cylindrical pore of length L under a uniform pressure drop, ∆p = p0 −pL. For each configuration, 104

Figure 2: Rejection (χ) as a function of the aspect ratio (λ = r/R) for single-pass transport of spherical particles through homogeneous cylindrical pores at different Péclet numbers (Pe), spanning diffusion- to convection-dominated regimes. Here, λ is prescribed directly as the ratio of a single representative particle radius r to pore radius R (i.e., a monodisperse suspension and a given membrane pore size), and no size distributions are considered. The solid red line denotes the sieving limit (Pe = 0), while the symbols show the impact of increasing Pe (0.5 ≤Pe ≤10). Hindered convection has the strongest impact in the intermediate range 0.2 ≤λ ≤0.6, where χ is enhanced by up to ≈15-20%.
Fig. 2 presents the rejection (χ) as a function of the aspect ratio (λ = r/R) for spherical particles in cylindrical pores under different Péclet numbers

Figure 3: Single heterogeneity ensemble construction. Top: two representative particle-pore size distributions drawn from the 104 independently sampled configurations. In each configuration, particle radii are heterogeneous, r ∼N(¯r, σr) with σr = 10 nm and ¯r scanned from 10 to 1000 nm (navy histogram), while pore radii are effectively monodisperse, R ∼N(500 nm, 0 nm) (magenta spike). Bottom: histogram of ¯λ over all 104 configurations; colored bands mark the ¯λ intervals used to group the ensemble in subsequent panels.

Figure 4: Single-heterogeneity transport response. (a) Configuration-averaged re- jection ¯χ versus configuration-averaged aspect ratio ¯λ across 104 independently sampled particle-pore configurations, (b) Ensemble histograms of Pe (top) and ¯χ (bottom); bars are colored by the same Pe intervals to connect transport regime to rejection outcome. Particles are polydisperse with r ∼N(¯r, σr), where ¯r is scanned from 10 to 1000 nm and σr = 10 nm, while pores are effectively monodisperse with R ∼N(500 nm, 0 nm). Parameters: ∆p = 10−2 Pa, L = 1.
